history.text: Around 1200, the first church, Saint Olaf's Church, was built.

history.label: 13th century
history.text: The oldest parts of the cathedral of Helsingør date back to the 13th century and show that the fishing village, as Helsingør was then, had grown to become a town of importance.
The city was first mentioned as Hælsingør and the people as Helsinger in King Valdemar the Victorious's Liber Census Daniæ from 1231 (not to be confused with the Helsings of Hälsingland in Sweden).

history.label: 1420s
history.text: The city as it is known today was founded in the 1420s by Danish King Eric of Pomerania.
He established the Sound Dues in 1429, which were a toll on the use of the Øresund.
The city's growing wealth further fuelled the growth of maritime transport through the Øresund Strait - from 1479 to 1581 the number of ships passing through the strait increased 6.5 times.

history.label: 1570s
history.text: Krogen was rebuilt and expanded in the 1570s by king Frederik II and renamed Kronborg.
In 1672, Helsingør had grown to be the third-largest town in Denmark.

history.label: 19th century
history.text: Up until the middle of the 19th century, Helsingør was among the largest cities in the country.
Adolf Hitler had ordered that all Danish Jews were to be arrested and deported to the concentration camps on Rosh HaShanah, the Jewish New Year, which fell on 2 October 1943.

history.label: Today
history.text: Place names show that the Helsinger may have had their main fort at Helsingborg and a fortified landing place at Helsingør, to control the ferry route across the strait.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: With that income, the king built a castle named Krogen or Ørekrog on the extreme northeastern tip of the island of Zealand.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The oldest known fortified building of Helsingør is Flynderborg, an early medieval fortress on a hill just south of the medieval city.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: During World War II, Helsingør was among the most important transport points for the rescue of Denmark's Jewish population during the Holocaust.
